posts.caption:
WEST’S SILENT OUTCRY
“It’s basically like the inside of my brain. It tells the story from when I was about 19 to now. It explores five different emotions, each just as intense as the others.
It’s called Man’s Ruin.” — Westislonely
Following the release of her new single ‘Bad Dog’ and ahead of a new EP due this July, Westislonely presents music rooted in the spirit of punk. Through striking black-and-white imagery, her music embodies the energy of rock, expressing her inner emotions with raw honesty.
The Westislonely full editorial will be available on our website soon.
Talent Westislonely @westisl0nely

posts.caption:
An artificial breath machine, phone incense holders, and the art of multitasking. @sansan_gear invited us to their Spring Summer 27 Press Preview, here’s what we found.
With a shifting focus towards merging organic and technical fabrics, San San Gear’s new collection “Soft Focus” is built around the idea of carving out a deliberate escape from the pressure of being everything to everyone at once. The clothes function as a kind of personal shield, and the space itself went all in on the concept. The showroom featured frosted glass throughout and an installation by German artist @realclemensfischer where a pneumatic belt slowly stretches and releases above you, timed to a countdown. Turning the subtle art of breathing many of us have come to take for granted into something we actually notice.
The tension between wanting to live on your own terms and constantly being pulled in every direction is not a new feeling, but San San Gear made a whole world out of it this season. With that, we think it’s a good brand to keep an eye on.
📌San San Gear SS27 Showroom Details
* Date: 23 - 26 June
* Location:  6 Rue Pecquay, 75004 Paris
Words by Levon Momdjian
📷 @a2z_fashionmag
#SanSanGear #SS27 #Menswear
#ParisFashionWeek #SoftFocus

posts.caption:
Another summer of sport is in full swing. While Formula 1 and the World Cup are drawing attention elsewhere, in London, all eyes are on Wimbledon. To mark the tournament, @miumiu and @newbalance have reunited with @cocogauff for their second collaboration. The capsule will be worn by Gauff on court at Wimbledon and is also available to shop.
At just 22, Gauff has already built an impressive career. The American tennis star has won eleven singles titles, reached number two in the WTA rankings, and become one of the most exciting names in the sport. Beyond her powerful serve and fearless backhand, she has also become known for bringing a strong sense of style to the tennis court. For Gauff, the collaboration is not just about wearing a beautiful kit. It is also about using tennis as a space for self-expression.
Wimbledon, however, comes with its own strict rules. Players are expected to wear almost entirely white, giving Gauff, New Balance and Miu Miu clear limits to work within. But instead of treating the dress code as a restriction, they used it as a starting point. The result is a more minimal and romantic take on tennis dressing, where soft details, clean silhouettes and performance fabrics come together on court.
This collection proves that Wimbledon whites are no longer just for the court. They are becoming part of fashion’s summer wardrobe too.Swipe to see the campaign and the collection.
Words by Josephine Jiang
Next-Level Fashion, Daily.
Follow @a2z_fashionmag Now.
📷@newbalance, @miumiu
#newbalance #miumiu #wimbledon #cocogauff

posts.caption:
Ahead of @louisvuitton’s SS27 menswear show, @pharrell teased a new sneaker through @skateboard, the account he often uses to preview key LV pieces before the runway.
The shoe arrives in red crocodile-embossed leather, with oversized white laces, metal eyelets and a clean white sole. Luxury materials, but a very familiar shape: the low-top silhouette immediately had people comparing it to the @vans Authentic.
Vans didn’t let the comparison go unnoticed. After commenting “ohhh bet,” the brand posted its own red Authentic with the caption: “Wanna know the time. Better clock us.” A pointed reference to Clipse’s Mr. Me Too, featuring Pharrell, a track known for calling out copycats.
Beyond the sneaker debate, Louis Vuitton’s surfboard-shaped teaser image also seems to hint at a broader coastal mood for SS27, suggesting themes of travel, leisure and ocean culture. It also feels like a subtle reference to Pharrell’s “Surfboard P” persona.
Swipe to see the teaser and let us know what you think.
Words by Josephine Jiang
Next-Level Fashion, Daily.
Follow @a2z_fashionmag Now.
📷@vans, @skateboard
#lv #pharrellwilliams #vans #louisvuitton #ss2027

posts.alt_text:
At the @prada Spring/Summer 2027 menswear show, a sharp focus on jeans – “the most universal garment” – proposed an aggressively narrow silhouette. Alexander Fury spoke to Miuccia Prada and Raf Simons backstage.⁠
⁠
Read @alexanderfury’s review at the link in bio 📲⁠
⁠
📸 #Prada Spring/Summer 2027 menswear⁠

posts.caption: A single idea, yeah. Singular? Not so much. I get that you can build on one single idea when creating something. That one idea has to be striking in itself. A pair of skinny jeans is just not. That may have been somewhat groundbreaking in the 1970s. Not today, I am afraid. It’s so funny how Prada tries to label it as an intellectual concept of reduction. Sorry, guys, didn’t work this time

posts.caption:
Some novels are entertaining, others thrilling or even shocking. But Pier Vittorio Tondelli’s Camere Separate, or Separate Rooms, has a haunting tone of its own. ⁠
⁠
Its austere-sounding title hides a beguiling and deliberately non-linear narrative that is confronting and revelatory. The novel’s protagonist, Leo, is an itinerant writer in his early thirties who is frozen by grief following the death of his younger partner, Thomas. The HIV/AIDS epidemic is never explicitly mentioned, but because of the era in which Separate Rooms unfolds, it feels almost impossible not to presume that Thomas has become one of its victims. Tondelli’s own life was cut short by AIDS in 1991, just two years after the novel was first published in his native Italian. ⁠
⁠
Now, a new English language reissue and forthcoming film adaption brings Pier Vittorio Tondelli’s novel Separate Rooms to a new generation of readers. @misternicklevine unpacks the book at the link in bio 📲⁠
⁠
📸 Courtesy of Sceptre⁠

posts.caption:
Death, female pleasure, the danse macabre, and Swan Lake are all sources of inspiration for @anne_imhof’s exhibition Citizen at @spruethmagers, London. ⁠
⁠
In conversation, the German artist and musician traces the through-lines of her work, from the Golden Lion-winning Faust at Venice in 2017 and her collaboration with American Ballet Theatre principal dancer Devon Teuscher, to the scratched Wave paintings and crowd barriers repurposed from her monumental DOOM at New York’s Park Avenue Armory last year.⁠
⁠
@sofia_hallstrom_ speaks with Imhof at the link in bio 📲⁠
⁠
📸 Lead image: Anne Imhof. Photography by @nad_1n3⁠
Installation views: Citizen, Sprüth Magers. Courtesy the artist and Sprüth Magers Photo: Ben Westoby / Fine Art Documentation

posts.caption:
For the @prada Spring/Summer 2027 menswear collection, instead of working with lots of different silhouettes and concepts, Miuccia Prada and Raf Simons did the opposite, working with only one. “We are a little bit anti. Anti-decoration. Anti-complication,” says Simons backstage before the show began, “We’re rejecting complication and decoration …” Mrs Prada adds: “It’s what we think is right.”
Swipe for @alexanderfury’s breakdown ➡️
📸#Prada Spring/Summer 2027 Menswear

posts.caption:
On the Thursday morning after the New York Knicks’ historic NBA finals comeback, @ryanmcginleystudios was busy installing his new exhibition at @jeffreydeitchgallery in SoHo. “New York is on fire right now,” the photographer tells @violetc0nroy, recalling the electric energy of the riots in the city the night before, when New Yorkers took to the streets celebrate the basketball team’s victory during a scorching heatwave. “Street energy is amazing, in any city.” ⁠
⁠
It’s an atmosphere that’s mirrored in McGinley’s photo series, Night Shift, which shows that city as you’ve never seen it before. “Doing this project reengaged me with the city that I’ve lived in for so long,” says the photographer. ⁠
⁠
Captured on gruelling night shifts from 9pm to 5am over the course of one year, the series draws a clear divide between the waking world – and its traditional 9-to-5 workers – and a parallel universe of nocturnal artists and their strange universe of unfettered creativity. “It’s about finding the beauty in the grime.”⁠
⁠
Read the interview at the link in bio 📲⁠
⁠
📸 Night Shift by Ryan McGinley. Courtesy of the artist and Jeffrey Deitch, New York and Los Angeles

posts.caption:
Harper’s Bazaar India digital cover star, Maye Musk (@mayemusk), started modelling as a teenager. She stopped at 22 to get married and raise three children, then picked it back up at 28—an age the industry considered, at the time, well past the point of no return. So she built her nutrition practice in parallel. By 31, she was in high demand as a model and continued part-time; in her 60s, she was doing high fashion. “If someone had told me as a teenager that I would be in Delhi for a top fashion magazine at 78, I would have thought they were crazy,” she says. “Yet, here I am, working alongside a very creative team.
Read the full cover story at the link in bio.
Editor: Rasna Bhasin (@rasnabhasin)
Interview: Geetika Sachdev (@geetikasachdev)
Photographer: Soujit Das (@soujit.das)
Stylist: Gopalika Virmani (@gopalikavirmani)
Cover Design: Mandeep Singh Khokhar (@mandy_khokhar19)
Editorial Coordinator: Shalini Kanojia (@shalinikanojia)
Make-up Artist: Itika Chugh (@fashionmakeupbyitikachugh)
Hair Artist: Umang Artist (@umang.artist), at Anima Creatives (@animacreatives)
Style Assistant: Grace Soni (@grace__soni_)
Maye Musk is wearing the Ethereal top with an abstract coral reef–inspired belt with sculptural organic textures, by Richa Khemka (@richakhemkalabel)
#HarpersBazaarIndia #MayeMusk
posts.alt_text:
From discussing masculinity and mindset to family, fashion, and finding comfort in authenticity, Jasprit Bumrah (@jaspritb1) keeps it refreshingly real in conversation with digital editor Sonal Ved (@sonalved). Speaking about everything from his love for fragrances and watches to switching off with family time, the ace cricketer proves that personal style is less about trends and more about individuality. He also shares why collaborating with Ralph Lauren Fragrances (@ralphlaurenfragrances) felt like a natural fit for his personal style and personality. Whether on the field or off duty, his approach remains grounded, thoughtful and deeply personal.

Tap the link in bio to watch the full video.

Styling: @eshaamiin1 

#BazaarIndia #HarpersBazaarIndia

posts.caption:
In an industry where surnames are inherited currencies, Bazaar India’s April-May cover star, Tabu (@tabutiful), has operated for over three decades on a mononym. To understand her career is to understand what it meant to say yes to things the industry was not ready for and to have the instinct to recognise those things as worthy, even when no template existed for them. But she refuses to call it rebellion. “It was simply a reflection of who I am. The roles I chose, the decisions I made—they came from some similar place throughout my life. Some part of me has been completely unchanged,” she insists.
Read the full cover story at the link in bio.
Editor: Rasna Bhasin (@rasnabhasin)
Interview: Malika Halder (@malikahalder)
Photographer: Soujit Das (@soujit.das)
Stylist: Gopalika Virmani (@gopalikavirmani)
Cover Design: Mandeep Singh Khokhar (@mandy_khokhar19)
Editorial Coordinator: Shalini Kanojia (@shalinikanojia)
Makeup Artist: Savleen Manchanda (@savleenmanchanda)
Hair Artist: Hiral Bhatia (@bbhiral)
Set Design: Nikita Rao (@nikita_315)
Assistant Stylist: Grace Soni (@grace__soni_)
Artist Management: Mili Savla (@milisavla)
Line Producer: Salim Memon (@lineproducer)
Tabu is wearing a shoulder-structured jacket from the Red Thin Line collection by Rajesh Pratap Singh (@rajeshpratapsinghworks), paired with the Colour Me Spring necklace, Eternal Knowing necklace, The Eternal Dance ring, and Flower of Life ring — all by Zoya Jewellery (@zoyajewels).
#HarpersBazaarIndia #TheBeautyIssue #Tabu

posts.caption:
‘Spider-Man: Brand New Day’ stars Zendaya (@zendaya) and Tom Holland (@tomholland2013) attended a fan event in Berlin ahead of the film’s release. For the evening event, Zendaya stepped out in a Louis Vuitton (@louisvuitton) black leather ensemble, while Holland wore a tailored black suit with a red shirt. Earlier in the day, Zendaya opted for a look from Coach (@coach)’s Fall/Winter 2026 runway, while Holland kept it casual in blue jeans and beige jacket.
📸: Getty Images
#BazaarIndia #HarpersBazaarIndia

posts.caption:
If there’s one accessory defining the season, it’s sporty sunglasses. Sharp silhouettes and tinted lenses are emerging as the newest statement piece, adding instant edge to even the simplest looks. Seen on Kim Kardashian and other A-listers, the look moves effortlessly from off-duty street style to elevated city dressing.
📸: Getty Images and Brands
#BazaarIndia #HarpersBazaarIndia
posts.alt_text:
Harper’s Bazaar India digital cover star, Maye Musk (@mayemusk), started modelling as a teenager. She stopped at 22 to get married and raise three children, then picked it back up at 28—an age the industry considered, at the time, well past the point of no return. So she built her nutrition practice in parallel. By 31, she was in high demand as a model and continued part-time; in her 60s, she was doing high fashion. “If someone had told me as a teenager that I would be in Delhi for a top fashion magazine at 78, I would have thought they were crazy,” she says. “Yet, here I am, working alongside a very creative team. 

Read the full cover story at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
People are returning to hobbies like journaling, pottery, knitting, painting, and boxing, embracing activities that don’t need to be shared online. In choosing hobbies that do not perform well online, many are rediscovering the simple pleasure of doing something purely because it makes them feel human. The most restorative activities today may be those that leave absolutely no digital trace.
Tap the link in bio to read on.
✍️: Anoushka Madan
📸: Spice, IMDb and Communique
#BazaarIndia #HarpersBazaarIndia

posts.caption:
“Sometimes I love to overdo everything and go crazy. I love bright colours: pink, orange, sunset colours, or the colours of beautiful flowers,” says @inkawilliams as a self-described minimalist, with maximalist tendencies. The French-Australian model is a vision of effortless beauty as she embodies the youthful spirit of the Chanel Rouge Coco Hydra Gloss, its frosted finishes, vivid colours and high-shine, hypnotising tubes.
Through the link in bio, see the beauty behind Inka Williams’ salty skin, flushed cheeks and reflective lips.
#InkaWilliams wears @chanel.beauty Rouge Coco Hydra Gloss and Summer Makeup Collection on the June 2026 digital cover of Harper’s BAZAAR Australia/New Zealand.
Words: @itsalexjduffy
Photography: @jamie_.green
Makeup: @victoriabaron using @chanel.beauty
Hair: @darrensummors
Styling: @rachelwayman
Fashion assistant: @kaileeewaller
Production director: @rebeccamooreproductions
Art director: @_cathryn.s.z
Digital director: @ariellekatos
posts.alt_text:
While Bali is ingrained in @inkawilliams’ life and sense of self, the beach is something she had to leave to love. “My mum used to force us to go to the beach. I remember hating it. I’m a Virgo . . . all the sand on my hands and my feet when I would go back to the car,” she tells BAZAAR. “Eventually, when I grew up and moved to London, I realised how much I need the beach. I don’t mind if it’s cold, but I need the sun wherever I am. I need nature. If I go too long without being in nature, I start to lose my mind.”

At the link in bio, Inka Williams details her connection to Bali and the daily rituals which keep her grounded far from home. 

#InkaWilliams wears @chanel.beauty Rouge Coco Hydra Gloss and Summer Makeup Collection on the June 2026 digital cover of Harper’s BAZAAR Australia/New Zealand.

posts.caption:
When home is somewhere between the island of Bali, Melbourne, Los Angeles, London and Paris, ‘home’ is a feeling @inkawilliams finds within herself. The Australian-born model was raised in Bali, where her childhood home remains an oasis from bustling city streets and long days on set. “I just love being at home,” says Williams. “The garden is just so beautiful. I feel like I’m living in a painting. Everything [in Bali] has developed around the house, but my house is still a bubble of green. I never want to leave home.”
Through the link in bio, the model and creative multihyphenate shares more on feeling at home across the world and with her love.
#InkaWilliams wears @chanel.beauty Rouge Coco Hydra Gloss and Summer Makeup Collection on the June 2026 digital cover of Harper’s BAZAAR Australia/New Zealand.

posts.caption:
Remember that modernist adage “less is more”? In an age of content abundance, a sharper lens — absolute, uncompromising — is a welcome thing. This sense of focus ran through the Milan men’s shows this season.
Read Angelo Flaccavento (@poeticallypunk)’s full report from Milan men’s fashion week where he covers  the radical reductionism at Prada and more. #linkinbio
📷 Spotlight/Launchmetrics.com
posts.alt_text:
It was only 10 months ago that the Los Angeles apparel brand Frame appeared at risk of becoming collateral damage in the open warfare between Erik Torstensson, one of its founders, and the venture capitalist Natalie Massenet, whose high-profile romance had ended in dueling lawsuits and weeks of lurid accusations that garnered unflattering headlines around the world. “Love, Drugs and a Fashion Breakup Worth $95 Million,” wrote The New York Times.

And yet the company now says it expects to reach new heights this year: According to Torstensson’s co-founder Jens Grede, Frame is on track to generate $300 million in sales this year as it prepares to open a three-story, 6,000-square-foot Paris flagship at 33 rue François 1er in the autumn and as many as 15 new stores in 2027.

Read the full story on how steering clear of a scandal put the label on track for $300M this year #linkinbio 

✍️ @ericwilsonsays 
📷 @frame

posts.caption:
Streaming live from Napa Valley, California on June 24 and 25, we are excited to announce that Bella Hadid, founder and chief creative officer of fragrance line ‘Ôrebella, will join the outstanding lineup of speakers at The Business of Beauty Global Forum 2026.
Hadid joins a stellar speaker slate including Guillaume Motte, president and CEO of Sephora, award-winning makeup artist Violette Serrat of Violette_FR, Byunghoon Kim, founder of the Korean juggernaut APR Corporation, home to Medicube, and Tom Hale, CEO of Oura. Faye Mythen, co-founder of Reborne Longevity, Alex Wiltschko, founder and CEO of AI company Osmo, author Emma Dabiri and Ôrebella CEO Anish Agarwal are new additions to our programming, as is fashion model and muse Paloma Elsesser, who will join us for an intimate conversation on beauty’s changing standards.
Sign up to the livestream to tune in #linkinbio
posts.alt_text:
Mara Roszak has always approached hair the way a sculptor approaches stone: less as a material to be manipulated than a form waiting to reveal itself.

The Los Angeles-based stylist, whose clients include Emma Stone and Zoe Saldaña, traces that philosophy back to her mother, who studied sculpture under the artist Chaim Gross, known for his wood and stone renderings of dancers and acrobats.

Roszak has emerged in the last two decades as the premier Hollywood hair stylist, known for her ability to find new, flattering styles on the likes of Stone, Saldaña and Michelle Yeoh. Her signature waves and cropped bobs seem to unlock a fresh but familiar geometry of beauty, as if it’s been there all along.

Read the full story by BoF's @brennankilbane #linkinbio 

📷 Kerry Crawford

posts.caption:
McQueen will return to London Fashion Week with a Spring-Summer 2027 runway show set for September 20th. “London is where our story began and remains central to our identity,” said CEO Gianfranco D’Attis.
The move strengthens London Fashion Week, adding another anchor to its September line up alongside Burberry and Christopher Kane’s Mulberry revamp.
Read the full story #linkinbio
✍️ @vikramkansara
📷 @alexandermcqueen

posts.caption:
It’s official, finally. Sunnei founders Simone Rizzo and Loris Messina are taking the creative reins at Moschino. The duo confirmed the appointment in an interview on Saturday after speculation that they were set to replace Adrian Appiolaza, who exited Aeffe-owned Moschino on Friday, grew louder. Their first show for Moschino will be held during Milan fashion week in September.
In a fashion world full of bizarre appointments, there’s a logic to the match. For a start, fashion is about provocative messaging as much as clothes these days, and the internet loves that most unfashionable of qualities: humour. Rizzo and Messina are fluent in both, serving up a sort of punk spirit alla Milanese: when the duo stepped down from Sunnei last year, they worked with Christie’s to stage a mock auction in which they put themselves and the brand up for sale.
Read the full story on the appointment by Angelo Flaccavento @poeticallypunk #linkinbio

Our cover star opts for cinema that sparks conversations, takes pride in her individuality, and fiercely protects her mental peace. “Today, there’s clarity in my choices. I’m shedding old versions of myself to make space for newer, riskier, more interesting ones,” she says, unfiltered, unassuming and refreshingly candid. Bhumi Satish Pednekkar welcomes Brides Today (@bhumisatishpednekkar) into her world of stylish experiments, socially resonant movies, sustainable choices, and the legacy she hopes to leave behind.

posts.caption: A Saint Laurent show always brings out the stars. Today in Paris, friends and longtime muses of @ysl including Connor Storrie, Charlie XCX, Kate Moss and more, attended the brand’s Spring-Summer 2027 men’s runway show. They of course all wore their sultriest looks for the occasion—swipe through to see them all.
posts.alt_text: From bold to neutral tones, relaxed fits and… a giant frog head, swipe through to see looks from @thombrowne, @canali, @tods and @giorgioarmani and @milanfashionweek men’s continues.

posts.alt_text: A Saint Laurent show always brings out the stars. Today in Paris, friends and longtime muses of @ysl including Connor Storrie, Charlie XCX, Kate Moss and more, attended the brand’s Spring-Summer 2027 men’s runway show. posts.caption:
Across five days every June, some 300,000 visitors descend on @ascotracecourse, just west of London, for the race meet, which dates back to 1711.
Each day opens with King Charles and Queen Camilla arriving in a horse-drawn carriage before withdrawing to the most exclusive viewing area, the Royal Enclosure. What sounds like a zoo for the exceedingly posh is an invitation-only section with an infamously strict dress code.
For centuries, spectacular hats have been woven into the social fabric of Royal Ascot. And the formal attire of the upper class continues to define the strict dress code today. Women are required to wear a headpiece in almost all the enclosures — though what exactly that looks like, depends on the class of ticket.
Inside the Royal Enclosure, it’s top hats and waistcoats for the gentlemen. For the ladies, hats or a headpiece with a base of at least 10 centimeters (3.9 inches). Fascinators — those bobbing bundles often of feathers, flowers and netting perched off-center of the wearer’s head — are banned here. Though they make an exception for children. In the more relaxed Queen Anne and Village Enclosures, smartly-dressed spectators get away with all kinds of outlandish headwear. And in the Windsor Enclosure, well, just about anything goes.
Though they do draw the line at sneakers.
📸: Kin Cheung/AP/Carl Court/Getty Images/Carlo Paloni/Jonathan Brady/David Davies/PA Wire/Justin Tallis/AFP/

posts.caption: Milan Men’s Fashion Week SS27 picked up where Pitti Uomo in Florence left off. There was a familiar yet fresh take on prep at @ralphlauren, an Italian summer through the lens of @dolcegabbana, the new Milanese uniform at @msgm, and a celebration of the collected life at @paulsmithdesign. Swipe through for some of our favorite scenes from this weekend’s shows.

posts.caption:
Dua Lipa is a @chanelofficial bride!
For the pop star’s whirlwind wedding weekend in Italy to actor Callum Turner earlier this month, Lipa wore the first Chanel Haute Couture wedding dress designed by creative director @matthieu_blazy. Handcrafted at the fashion house’s ateliers in Paris, the entirely hand-embroidered gown was constructed of a whopping 480,000 beads and required 1,155 hours of needlework. The stunning bridal creation also featured a two-metre train, 25,000 wispy feathers, and a floor-grazing tulle wedding veil.
📸: courtesy of @chanelofficial / @davidsimsofficial
posts.alt_text: A Saint Laurent show always brings out the stars. Today in Paris, friends and longtime muses of @ysl including Connor Storrie, Charlie XCX, Kate Moss and more, attended the brand’s Spring-Summer 2027 men’s runway show. They of course all wore their sultriest looks for the occasion—swipe through to see them all.

posts.caption:
Simone Rocha made her menswear show debut at @pittiuomo_official in Florence on Thursday evening. While the temperature was high outside the Teatro Della Pergola, inside, the scene was cool and collected as guests took to the stage to find their seats in the round. This was a show inspired and fueled by the notion of performance. Rocha’s skill in celebrating classical menswear codes—tailoring, shirting, and Oxford brogues—were insouciantly fused with the romantic flourishes that she has built her brand DNA on. (Pussybow blouses, floral embroidery anglaise shirts, pinafores, ballet flats, and aprons!) @simonerocha_ called it “a grand tour,” and one that celebrated every age of man through her light touch and romantic lens.
“There is knowing innocence to proportions that nod to different ages of man—childhood, adolescence, adulthood,” read the show notes. “The space in which we convene also leaves its trace on this collection, a reaction to place and time. Floral patterns are drawn from the linings of costume trunks, a Florentine scene becomes a souvenir print, airs of the theatre universe animate silhouettes with a languid swagger, within this ancient city mythologized through art and literature.”

posts.alt_text:
There are influencers, and then there’s @madelineargy. Since 2021, the “Pretty Lonesome” host has made eight million strangers feel like her closest friends without even trying. Whether she’s giving advice on toxic friendships or crashing out over Jane Goodall, it’s clear that Argy’s got range—and so does her closet.

Coveteur went inside the closet of her LA home and found rows of candy-colored @chanelofficial bags on one wall and thrifted tanks from @thredup and @depop on the other.

Tap the link in bio for our latest #ClosetTour to step inside her world, from the breakup that led to her most-treasured boots to why she’ll defend backpacks until the very end.

posts.caption:
Few figures have left a mark on fashion and popular culture quite like Edie Sedgwick.
With her cropped silver-blonde hair, oversized chandelier earrings, and effortless sense of style, she became one of the defining faces of 1960s New York. More than a socialite, actress, or model, Edie embodied a certain kind of magnetism that couldn’t be manufactured. She didn’t just influence fashion—she became part of its mythology.
After arriving in New York, Sedgwick quickly became a fixture of Andy Warhol’s Factory, the creative epicenter of the city’s underground art scene. Warhol recognized her charisma almost immediately, casting her in several of his films and transforming her into the unofficial star of the Factory. Together, they created an image that would define an era: part glamour, part rebellion, part beautiful chaos.
What made Edie so captivating was her ability to appear both untouchable and vulnerable at the same time. She moved through New York’s cultural circles like a shooting star, becoming a muse not only to artists and filmmakers, but to fashion itself. Decades later, her influence can still be seen everywhere—from modern It-girls and indie sleaze aesthetics to the enduring fascination with downtown cool.
Yet behind the flashbulbs and attention was a far more complicated reality. Struggling with addiction, mental health issues, and the pressures of sudden fame, Sedgwick’s life began to unravel almost as quickly as it had risen. She died in 1971 at just 28 years old, leaving behind a legacy that remains both inspiring and heartbreaking.
More than fifty years later, Edie Sedgwick continues to symbolize a particular kind of beauty—one that is imperfect, spontaneous, and impossible to replicate. Not simply an icon, but a legend whose story still haunts fashion, art, and New York itself.
posts.alt_text:
Between 1998 and 2002, photographer Norbert Schoerner helped redefine the visual language of the brand, creating a series of campaigns that remain some of the most distinctive in fashion history. At a time when luxury advertising often relied on glamour, fantasy, and polished perfection, Schoerner moved in the opposite direction.

His Prada was strange, clinical, and deliberately unsettling.

Working with recurring faces such as Angela Lindvall, Schoerner abandoned conventional beauty photography in favor of something more observational. Extreme close-ups, visible skin texture, awkward gestures, harsh lighting, and sterile environments transformed the campaigns into studies of human behavior rather than traditional advertisements. Models were no longer distant ideals but subjects under scrutiny.

What made the work feel so revolutionary was its embrace of imperfection. Nail biting, absent-minded expressions, dirt-covered hands, and moments that would normally be edited out became central elements of the imagery. The result captured the tension between beauty and discomfort that has long defined Prada’s identity.

Visually, the campaigns also reflected a unique moment in time. The late 1990s and early 2000s saw analogue and digital technologies collide, and Schoerner’s images embraced that transition. Rather than hiding technological imperfections, the campaigns often amplified them, creating photographs that felt futuristic, fragmented, and slightly disorienting.

More than twenty years later, the influence of this period can still be seen throughout fashion photography. The psychological distance, the artificial environments, and the fascination with unconventional beauty all became hallmarks of modern Prada imagery.

In 2024, Schoerner’s work for the house was compiled in PRADA ARCHIVE 1998–2002, a publication that reaffirmed the significance of these campaigns within the brand’s history. Looking back, they feel less like advertisements and more like pieces of contemporary art—evidence of a moment when Prada wasn’t simply selling clothes, but reshaping how fashion could be seen.

posts.caption:
Fashion after dark.
This editorial captures the chaos, glamour, and excess of nightlife.
Created by Steven Klein for the magazine’s February 2017 issue, the story unfolds like a fever dream of fashion, celebrity, and self-invention. Shot through the raw lens of Polaroid photography, the editorial blurs the line between reality and performance, transforming its cast into larger-than-life characters suspended somewhere between a downtown club, an underground art scene, and a fantasy world of their own.
What makes the editorial so compelling is its sense of unpredictability. Beauty becomes exaggerated, identities become fluid, and glamour is pushed to its most theatrical extreme. Glitter-covered faces, dramatic silhouettes, fur coats, latex, couture, and eccentric styling collide in a series of images that feel both nostalgic and futuristic at the same time.
As one of the final projects developed under Franca Sozzani’s vision for Vogue Italia, The Polaroid Issue celebrates the kind of fearless creativity that defined the magazine for decades. Rather than documenting fashion, it creates an entire universe around it—one where excess, fantasy, and individuality are not only welcomed, but celebrated.
Nearly a decade later, the editorial still feels strikingly contemporary. In an era of carefully curated perfection, its messy glamour, artistic freedom, and unapologetic weirdness feel more relevant than ever.
Editorial: The Polaroid Issue
Magazine: Vogue Italia, February 2017
Photographer: Steven Klein
Fashion Editors/Stylists: Patti Wilson, Arianne Phillips, Carlyne Cerf de Dudzeele
Hair: Ward Stegerhoek, Shay Ashual
Makeup: Sammy Mourabit, Yadim
Casting: Angus Munro, Noah Shelley
Manicure: Honey
Set Design: Jesse Kaufmann
Featured Models: Achok Majak, Anna Cleveland, Anna Ewers, Barbara Fialho, Caroline Vreeland, Christina Kruse, Coco Rocha, Crystal Renn, Dilone, Ebony Riley, Erin O’Connor, Guinevere van Seenus, Jasmine Sanders, Jordan Barrett, Li Xiao Xing, Nataša Vojnović, Stella Lucia and others.

posts.caption:
Few figures have shaped fashion as profoundly as André Leon Talley.
Long before diversity became a talking point, Talley occupied spaces in an industry that was far less diverse than it is today.
Rising from Durham, North Carolina, to the highest ranks of the fashion industry, he became one of the most influential voices of his generation, serving as Creative Director and later Editor-at-Large at Vogue. Throughout his career, he combined an encyclopedic knowledge of fashion history with an unmistakable personal style, becoming as recognizable as the designers and models he championed.
But Talley’s influence extended far beyond magazines. He acted as a bridge between fashion, culture, art, and history, mentoring generations of creatives while advocating for greater visibility within an industry that often excluded Black voices. His presence helped redefine who could participate in luxury fashion and who could shape its narrative.
What made Talley extraordinary was his sense of scale. Everything about him felt larger than life: the sweeping capes, the custom caftans, the booming voice, and the passion with which he spoke about fashion. Yet behind the grandeur was a deep intellectual curiosity and an unwavering belief that fashion mattered. not merely as clothing, but as culture.
Today, years after his passing, his legacy continues to resonate. The 2026 exhibition André Leon Talley: Le Style est Éternel celebrates not only his remarkable wardrobe but also the influence he had on generations of designers, editors, models, and fashion lovers around the world. More than an editor, more than an insider, André Leon Talley was a cultural force.
Fashion has produced many icons.
But only one Godfather.

posts.caption:
Long before boho returned to fashion’s agenda, Gucci Fall/Winter 2008 was already doing it at its most luxurious. Under Frida Giannini, the house embraced a vision of glamour that was rich, romantic, and unapologetically excessive.
Velvet jackets, flowing dresses, jewel tones, ornate embellishments, fur accents, and layers of gold jewelry moved down the runway with effortless confidence. The collection drew from the spirit of the jet set, blending seventies bohemianism with old-world opulence and rock-and-roll glamour.
What makes the collection feel so relevant today is its refusal to be understated. Every look celebrates individuality, fantasy, and the pleasure of dressing up. Nothing is quiet, nothing is restrained, and that’s precisely what makes it so compelling.
In an era increasingly defined by minimalism, Gucci FW08 offered the opposite: a wardrobe built on drama, texture, and personality. It wasn’t just about clothes—it was about creating a character.
More than fifteen years later, the collection remains a reminder that fashion can be maximalist, emotional, and glamorous all at once. Perhaps that’s why so many designers and consumers are looking back at this era again. Sometimes, more really is more.
Collection: Gucci Fall/Winter 2008 Womenswear
Creative Director: Frida Giannini
Fashion House: Gucci
Presented during: Milan Fashion Week

posts.caption:
That question sits at the heart of „Brilliantly British“, a British Vogue editorial that transformed some of fashion’s biggest names into a cast of polished rebels. Featuring Kate Moss alongside a new generation of models, the story plays with the visual language of punk, leather, tartan, studs, ripped fabrics, heavy boots, and deliberately undone beauty looks, while placing it firmly within the world of high fashion.
It’s a contradiction that makes the editorial so compelling. Punk was born as a rejection of convention, yet here it is reinterpreted through the pages of one of fashion’s most influential magazines. What once existed outside the system becomes part of it. Rebellion becomes aesthetic. Anti-fashion becomes fashion.
Rather than documenting punk as it was, Brilliantly British explores punk as a cultural symbol—one that continues to be borrowed, reinvented, and commercialized decades after its emergence. The result is less about authenticity and more about the enduring appeal of attitude. Because whether fashion truly understands punk or not, it has never stopped being fascinated by it.
And perhaps that’s why the editorial still resonates today. It raises a question that remains surprisingly relevant: if everyone can dress like a punk, what does being punk actually mean?
Editorial: Brilliantly British
Magazine: British Vogue
Photographer: Tim Walker
Stylist/Fashion Editor: Lucinda Chambers
Models: Kate Moss, Lily Donaldson, Georgia May Jagger, Cara Delevingne, Edie Campbell, Karen Elson, Stella Tennant, Jourdan Dunn and others
Hair: Sam McKnight
Makeup: Val Garland

posts.caption:
💎 THE FUTURE OF DIAMONDS, IN THEIR WORDS
An exclusive Diamond World series featuring perspectives from the official panelists of the 41st World Diamond Congress, Singapore 🇸🇬 |12–15 July 2026.
The future of natural diamonds isn’t just about consumers. It’s about future talent. 💎
As a second-generation diamantaire, Molefi Letsiki reminds us that preserving the industry’s legacy goes beyond protecting a product — it’s about creating opportunities for the next generation.
Because the future of the diamond trade will depend on our ability to preserve its foundations while embracing new perspectives.
💬 How can the diamond industry become more appealing to the next generation?
#DiamondWorld #DiamondIndustry #NaturalDiamonds #FutureOfDiamonds #NextGenLeadership
posts.alt_text:
💎 THE FUTURE OF DIAMONDS, IN THEIR WORDS

An exclusive Diamond World series featuring perspectives from the official panelists of the 41st World Diamond Congress, Singapore 🇸🇬 |12–15 July 2026.

The future of the diamond industry isn’t a supply question. It’s a succession question. 💎

David Troostwijk highlights a conversation the industry can no longer postpone: How do we make the diamond industry more appealing, accessible and relevant for the next generation?

From onboarding challenges to generational integration, the next era of growth may depend on how successfully today’s leaders create opportunities for tomorrow’s professionals.

💬 What’s the biggest barrier preventing young professionals from entering the diamond industry today?

#DiamondWorld #DiamondIndustry #YoungDiamantaires #DavidTroostwijk #FutureOfDiamonds

posts.caption:
Did fancy cuts just become the new status symbol?
The signals appeared first at the Met Gala 2026.
Then JCK reinforced the same message.
From ovals and pears to marquise, emerald and elongated cushions, shape emerged as one of the strongest conversations in the diamond market.
While round diamonds still account for approximately 62% of sales, retailers and suppliers are reporting growing momentum around fancy shapes that offer individuality, visual size advantage and stronger design identity.
The question is no longer whether fancy cuts are trending.
The question is which shapes will define the next phase of diamond demand.
Save this for your buying strategy.
Share it with your team.
#DiamondWorld #DiamondPulse #JCK2026 #FancyCutDiamonds #NaturalDiamonds

posts.caption:
For years, diamond brooches were seen as ceremonial pieces with limited relevance.
That perception is changing.
From red carpet styling and wedding wear to men’s jewellery and multifunctional designs, brooches are re-emerging as one of the most underleveraged categories in fine jewellery retail.
Why this matters for jewellers:
• Offers strong design differentiation
• Appeals to younger, style-conscious buyers
• Works across multiple occasions and wardrobes
• Enhances perceived value through craftsmanship
• Creates fresh storytelling opportunities
The biggest shift? Consumers no longer see brooches as one-dimensional ornaments.
They see them as versatile statement pieces that can be worn on blazers, saris, gowns, bags, hats, and even transformed into pendants or hair accessories.
In a market crowded with rings, earrings, and necklaces, brooches offer retailers a chance to stand out.
Sometimes, the biggest opportunities are hidden in categories the industry has overlooked.
Do you believe diamond brooches are ready for a full-fledged comeback?
Share your thoughts below.
#DiamondWorld #DWMagazine #DiamondBrooch #JewelleryTrends #JewelleryRetail

posts.caption:
For years, diamonds were marketed as symbols of eternity.
Botswana just showed the world they can also symbolize movement, achievement, and social impact.
At the 2026 World Athletics Relays, every gold medal featured a traceable natural diamond mined, cut, and polished in Botswana.
That single decision reframed the diamond narrative.
What This Means for the Industry
💎 Provenance is becoming a competitive advantage.
💎 Diamonds can showcase measurable community impact.
💎 Producer nations must retain more value through beneficiation.
💎 Natural diamonds gain strength when linked to authentic stories.
💎 Consumers increasingly reward transparency over mythology.
The Strategic Shift
The future of natural diamonds lies in connecting:
Origin + Ethics + Purpose + Storytelling
Because the most valuable diamond isn’t just beautiful.
It proves where it came from and what it made possible.
As Dr M’zée Fula Ngenge, Chairman of the African Diamond Council, puts it:
“A diamond at its best is not a possession, but a promise.”
What role can diamonds play beyond jewellery?
#NaturalDiamonds #DiamondWorld #DiamondIndustry #DWMagazine #DiamondsWithPurpose
posts.alt_text:
💎 THE FUTURE OF DIAMONDS, IN THEIR WORDS

An exclusive Diamond World series featuring perspectives from the official panelists of the 41st World Diamond Congress, Singapore 🇸🇬 |12–15 July 2026.

The future of natural diamonds isn’t just about consumers. It’s about future talent. 💎

As a second-generation diamantaire, Molefi Letsiki reminds us that preserving the industry’s legacy goes beyond protecting a product — it’s about creating opportunities for the next generation.

Because the future of the diamond trade will depend on our ability to preserve its foundations while embracing new perspectives.

💬 How can the diamond industry become more appealing to the next generation?

#DiamondWorld #DiamondIndustry #NaturalDiamonds #FutureOfDiamonds #NextGenLeadership

posts.caption:
The future of natural diamonds won’t be won through supply alone.
It will be won through meaning.
ALROSA’s newly approved 2030 promotion strategy signals a major shift in focus — from production and pricing to storytelling, education, transparency and consumer confidence.
The question is no longer:
“Can the industry sell more diamonds?”
It’s:
“Can the industry communicate why they matter?”
💬 What will drive natural diamond demand the most over the next decade?
#DiamondWorld #NaturalDiamonds #ALROSA #DiamondIndustry #DiamondRetail

posts.caption:
Madonna has officially confirmed that her long-awaited, self-authored biopic was dropped due to disagreements over budgeting and film locations.
“We had a falling out, me and Universal, regarding budget because I needed—I’ve had an extraordinary life. I’ve had a huge life, so I needed a big budget,” the pop star said in a Q&A with Interview magazine published yesterday alongside a theatrical set of photos by Nadia Lee Cohen.
Universal doesn’t seem to understand who they were working with. Madonna claimed that despite the production company’s skepticism, she’d found a way to “make [the film] for less money in Serbia” but they still didn’t believe she could pull it off.
“One of their first reactions was, ‘We don’t believe you’d stay in Serbia more than four days.’ And I said, ‘Did you read the script?’ My whole life has been survival. I’m not going there for a holiday.”
Through with Universal’s antics, Madonna attempted to buy her own script back from Universal for an “extortionist’s price.”
Madonna also confirmed that she worked with Netflix on turning the idea into a series. She, a seasoned film star and cinephile, admitted that series are a different breed entirely, and that she found herself dizzied by the process. Unable to find a writer and a showrunner for the project, she turned back to music; And that is how her upcoming album ‘Confessions on a Dance Floor: Part II’ was born.
“I thought the world is in a very dark place,” Madonna said, “and people need to dance.”
Indeed it is a dark world where even Madonna can’t get a biopic made because one of the world’s top production companies is too busy throwing money at films like ‘The Super Mario Galaxy Movie.’ Nevertheless, the star went on to share the amazing story of her early career at Danceteria — a story that is highlighted in her new song of the same name, and which is also memorialized in her new short film ‘Confessions II - The Film.”
The Danceteria story is, in itself, even without ornamentation, a feature-length pitch for the ages. Maybe someone with a good head on their shoulders will hear it and get to work on the biopic. But, until that happens, who wants to go dancing?
posts.alt_text:
Yesterday, Olivia Rodrigo unveiled Daisy Chain Fields Festival and the all-women lineup is giving major Lilith Fair energy. Taking over Irvine, CA this summer, the fest is stacked with heavy hitters, including Rodrigo herself alongside Bikini Kill, Chappell Roan, Doechii, Katseye, and many more. And if that wasn’t enough, the list also features special guests Karen O, Sarah McLachlan, and Stevie Nicks (!!!)

“Truly never felt more excited to share a piece of news with you all,” the singer shared on Instagram. “I’ve had a dream of doing this festival for years and i am so ecstatic its finally coming true!! Daisy Chain Fields features an all-women lineup and 100 percent of the net proceeds will go to charities dedicated to advancing and advocating for women and girls.”

According to the festival’s website, net proceeds will benefit non-profit organizations like Black Mamas Matter Alliance, Johns Hopkins Center for Indigenous Health, National Domestic Workers Alliance, Planned Parenthood, and several others.

“I firmly believe that joy, community, and music can be the drivers of meaningful change and I’m hopeful this festival will be just that,” Rodrigo added. “I absolutely cannot wait to scream and dance and sing with you guys August 29th!!!!”

#oliviarodrigo #music #singer #festival #lilithfair

posts.caption:
Yesterday, Andrea Marshall, the designer behind Salon 1884, was accused by Manhattan District Attorney Alvin L. Bragg, Jr. of running a sweatshop in NYC’s Garment District. Marshall faces felony charges of grand larceny, scheme to defraud, and failure to secure payment, as well as a misdemeanor charge for failure to pay wages. She has pleaded not guilty.
“As alleged, Andrea Marshall, the owner of Salon 1884, exploited and stole approximately $54,000 in wages from her employees while selling luxury garments,” the District Attorney said. “She preyed on the significant power imbalance of her employees who relied on the promised wages for housing and basic living expenses, feared retaliation, and lacked the financial resources to pursue legal recourse.”
He went on to describe a recurring cycle in which Marshall would “berate” workers and “scream at them” before periods of non-payment.
“And that’s on top of grueling hours, up to 17 hour days, and up to 100 hour work weeks,” the District Attorney added.
Marshall is alleged to have withheld payment for 924 work hours accrued by nine workers. Not only did she fail to pay them for these hours, but she withheld compensation even after the workers obtained small claims judgments. Text messages show one of the workers begging Marshall for payment, explaining how she needed to send funds to Ecuador for her sick son.
Marshall’s misconduct is not isolated to garment production workers, but extends to contractors and freelancers who worked with Salon 1884 such as “modeling agencies, a pattern maker, and a hair stylist.” She also neglected to make payments to worker’s compensation insurance.
Marshall, despite yesterday’s court proceedings, posted six times on her personal Instagram to promote a Salon 1884 online archive sale. Ironically, Salon 1884’s website touts “exceptional craftsmanship and sustainable practices” and says that garments are “responsibly made with environmentally-conscious materials and manufactured in The United States in New York City’s Garment Center.”
Guess it just goes to show you really can’t assume anything about pricing equating to fair labor in fashion!

posts.caption:
This week, Snap Inc. announced their latest contribution to the deeply flawed and highly polemical world of augmented reality/artificial intelligence glasses. The tech giant is leaning on fashion to sell the $2,195 hardware, tapping photographer Steven Meisel for a sleek launch campaign.
However, photos of Snap Inc. CEO Evan Spiegel wearing the glasses IRL have all but completely derailed any of the cool-factor the campaign may have drummed up.
Snap admits that building a computer into a cool, lightweight pair of glasses is not an easy task. In their press release, they cite the “isolating and cumbersome” nature of tech glasses and headgear that exists on the market today. However, with the ultimate goal of wearability in mind, Snap has utilized “advanced technology found in Boeing 787 Dreamliner windows” for one of several layers of lenses packed into their featherweight Swiss TR90 polymer frames.
In other words, you’ll be lugging a commercial airliner, a whole computer, and some sporty dad goggles on your face all at the same time.
Notable features include interactive AI and overlaying screens. With these tools, Snap boasts that you can “transform almost any place into a workspace.” Because that’s exactly what the people need: A thinner barrier between our professional lives and every single other aspect of our lives.
But the most controversial aspect of this tech in general is the privacy element, especially as it relates to built-in cameras. Meta’s model has been marred with issues relating to its cameras streaming private footage to subcontractors in Kenya, for example. Snap claims that their glasses, “ask clearly before accessing sensitive information, and an LED indicator lights up when recording is taking place,” but Snap does not go into further detail about how the glasses might ask and acquire consent from its wearer, or anyone else for that matter.
All that said, the most pressing question on everyone’s mind is: Will Snap’s glasses allow you to project Snapchat filters onto everyone around you? The answer remains unclear.

posts.caption:
EXCUSE????? What are the men using????
The modern man has come a long way from hunting wild boar and gathering berries. His greatest prey now is a Korean exfoliating acid with a cult following and a waiting list. Once upon a time men changed governments. Now they change skincare routines.
Pigmentation, sebum, enlarged pores, fine lines. The enemies of the state have changed. Somewhere along the way they discovered that looking handsome adds considerable weight to an opinion. Mansplaining, but with luminosity. A little easier to digest when delivered through glass skin. Every evening, across the world, men stand in front of mirrors dragging serum droppers across their foreheads with the concentration of Renaissance painters restoring a chapel ceiling.
Nothing feminine about looking good, darling. Vanity has always been a masculine pastime. The Greeks knew it. The dandies knew it. The peptide-using gymshark-wearing gym bros accidentally rediscovered it.
No homo. Just metro. Metrosexual. A term so gloriously of its time it deserves preservation in a museum.

posts.caption:
Arjun in Zindagi Na Milegi Dobara was a portrait of the workaholic millennial (are they okay??). It was meant to expose the absurdity of a high-profile corporate life that demanded constant availability. The kind of life tethered to every corner of the world and dictated by schedules across time zones. Watching it in 2011, you thought: who joins a client video call in the middle of a road trip? You nodded along with Laila, “Insaan ko dibbe mein sirf tab hona chahiye jab woh mar chuka ho.”
Do you laugh at your own naïveté now that your vacation has turned into eat, pray, Teams?
Once a physical location, the office is now something far more elusive. Stepping out of an office door meant shutting shop for the day. Work was confined by actual walls. But now that your phone is practically your conjoined twin, clocking out feels more like a corporate myth. Physical accessibility is no longer a limitation. Whether you’re on a beach in the Maldives or at a breakfast buffet in a Hyatt, any place can become a conference room if you put your mind to it. The only holiday epiphany you’re likely to have is the realisation that somewhere between your employment contract and your inbox, you signed your soul and calendar away.
DISCUSS ☕️✍🏽

posts.caption:
#ELLEDigitalCoverStar: There is a version of Kirandeep Chahal’s (@kirandeep_chahal) backstory that will hit close to home for many Indian women. To come from a family that cannot comprehend the desire for a life earned and lived on your own terms. To be expected to get married young, start a family, and follow a pre-written narrative folded neatly into the same shape it has always taken. Then there is the version she followed instead, one that eventually led her to becoming a familiar fixture on the runways of Dior, Dries Van Noten and Rick Owens, among other names that make fashion disciples starstruck.
Head to the 🔗 in bio to read the cover story.
On @kirandeep_chahal: LOVE ring in white gold & diamonds, LOVE bracelet in white gold, sapphires, & diamonds, LOVE bracelet in rose gold, pink sapphires & diamonds, LOVE bracelet in yellow gold, tsavorite garnets & diamonds, all by @cartier. Mala top by @raw_mango. Miss Z Riviera heels by @christianlouboutin.

#ELLEDigitalCoverStar: There is a version of Kirandeep Chahal’s (@kirandeep_chahal) backstory that will hit close to home for many Indian women. To come from a family that cannot comprehend the desire for a life earned and lived on your own terms. To be expected to get married young, start a family, and follow a pre-written narrative folded neatly into the same shape it has always taken. Then there is the version she followed instead, one that eventually led her to becoming a familiar fixture on the runways of Dior, Dries Van Noten and Rick Owens, among other names that make fashion disciples starstruck.
Head to the 🔗 in bio to read the cover story. 
.
On @kirandeep_chahal: LOVE ring in white gold & diamonds, LOVE bracelet in white gold, sapphires, & diamonds, LOVE bracelet in rose gold, pink sapphires & diamonds, LOVE bracelet in yellow gold, tsavorite garnets & diamonds, all by @cartier. Mala top by @raw_mango. Miss Z Riviera heels by @christianlouboutin. 
.
Editorial Director: Ainee Nizami Ahmedi (@aineenizamiahmedi)
Photographer: Noémi Ottilia Szabo (@noemiottiliaszabo)
Jr Fashion Stylist: Tejashree Raul (@tejashreeraul)

posts.caption:
#ELLECoverStar: There is a version of Zinnia Kumar (now Rose) (@zinniakumar) that fashion understands easily. The model. An image that moves seamlessly between Paris, New York, and wherever the industry gathers next.
It is also the least interesting version of her.
We’re at Le Bristol in Paris and the day carries all the familiar markers of a luxury cover shoot. Controlled light. Precise framing. Cartier anchoring the narrative. But Rose doesn’t perform that script in the expected way. She arrives ready, slips into each look without negotiation, barely checks the monitor. There is no sense of overworking the image, only an instinctive understanding of how to move within it. Head to the 🔗 in bio to read our conversation with the cover star.
On @zinniakumar: Clash de Cartier earrings in rose gold and green dyed agate, Clash de Cartier necklace in rose gold, Clash de Cartier ring in rose gold and chalcedony, Clash De Cartier ring in rose gold and onyx, and Clash de Cartier ring in rose gold and red dyed agate, all by @cartier. Textured draped top paired and draped trousers both by @newbottega
Editorial Director and words by: Ainee Nizami Ahmedi (@aineenizamiahmedi)
Photographer: Noémi Ottilia Szabo (@noemiottiliaszabo)
Stylist: Rhia Kapoor (@rhiakapoor)

posts.caption:
Presenting our first of four Summer Issue cover stars, @annehathaway.
Due to filming and release delays, Hathaway now has five movies premiering within six months. Her insanely busy year continues with her starring role as Penelope in @theodysseymovie. (To which she says: “It’s crazy to have four years of work coming out in six months.”) After weathering the storms of Hollywood, the in-demand star is finally collecting her flowers—and stopping to smell them. “If I’m not enjoying it, then really, really what’s wrong with me?” she tells ELLE. “If I can’t have a positive outlook on it when I’m having such a fortunate ride, then it would be wasted on me.”
For ELLE’s Summer 2026 issue, Hathaway reflects on how she’s grown since the early days of her career, reuniting with Christopher Nolan after 12 years, and navigating online criticism: “We’re at a time when people feel very confident in assuming what they think is fact.” Read the full cover story at the link in bio.
ELLE: @elleusa
Editor-in-chief: Nina Garcia @ninagarcia
Photographer: Norman Jean Roy @normanjeanroy
Stylist: Law Roach @luxurylaw
Writer: Kayla Webley Adler @kaylaw
Hair: Orlando Pita at Orlo Salon @hairbyorlandopita @orlosalon
Makeup: Fulvia Farolfi at MA+ Group @fulviafarolfi @maworldgroup
Manicure: Deborah Lippmann for Deborah Lippmann @thelippmannofficial @thedeborahlippmann
Set design: Marla Weinhoff at 11th House Agency @marlaweinhoff @11thhouseagency
Produced by Alexey Galetskiy Productions @alexeyg
posts.alt_text: From a by-appointment-only studio in Brooklyn, where @savettenewyork continues to refine its cult-favorite accessories, to milliner @paulakasu’s thoughtful reimagining of hat-making , creativity takes center stage this summer. Across the art world, a major James Turrell exhibition illuminates the @arosartmuseum, while “Yves Saint Laurent and Photography” offers a fresh look at the designer’s enduring legacy. Swipe through for the cultural moments, designers, and destinations capturing our attention this season, as seen in the pages of ELLE’s Summer Issue.

posts.caption:
In recent years, skin care routines have gotten supercharged, thanks mostly to tools. After all, the skin care tool market, already valued at more than $76 billion, is expected to grow by 53 percent by 2030. As demand continues to grow for skin gadgets, it’s even harder to figure out which ones work and which ones just sound impressive.
But you're in luck; for ELLE's third annual Tools and Treatments Awards, we recruited 28 staffers to put some of the most popular tools to the test, to see if they could help sculpt, lift, and tone. We asked for brutal honesty: Did the LED mask they tried make a difference or simply look futuristic? Was their face more sculpted after using microcurrent, or did it look the same? Did the TikTok-famous multimodal tool live up to the hype, or was it just that—hype? At the link in bio, we got the answers to all of these questions and more, and found the best skin care tools that are actually worth adding to your routine.

posts.caption:
At the 2026 @goldenglobes, Claire Danes brought high fashion to an everyday staple in a custom @gapstudio T-shirt gown. Now, @zacposen is bringing that red carpet glamour to the masses.
The brand has released a limited-edition capsule collection, pulling inspiration from Danes’s custom look and @gap’s signature pocket tee. The capsule translates the dramatic proportions and thoughtful construction of the original gown into two wearable pieces: a scoop-back T-shirt and a maxi dress. Both styles retain signature Gap details, including the chest pocket and rollable sleeves, while elevating them through refined silhouettes and premium materials.
Comment “t-shirt” to shop now and tap the link in bio to learn more.

posts.caption:
Fresh off his powerhouse turn in Bison Kaalamaadan, cover star Dhruv Vikram (@dhruv.vikram) is entering a defining new phase. The son of superstar Vikram quite literally wears his legacy under his sleeve, with a tattoo of his father’s name on his wrist. Still showing flashes of the little boy who grew up believing he was a hero too, he speaks to us about ambition, expectation and the long road to becoming his own man.

posts.caption:
When John Travolta took to berets at Cannes 2026, it was a statement of distinction—between his career as an actor and his new role as a filmmaker. To author AM Gautam, the beret is a “totemic thing” that convinces him of the “story” that he’s a writer. To wear one is to announce that you answer to an older and higher court than the present. From Rembrandt and Pablo Picasso to the Black Panther Party and closer home, figures like Dev Anand, and the late poet Dilip Chitre—it’s been a unique instrument of self-fashioning.
Written by: Diya Isha (@contendish)
#EsquireIndia #FashionHistory #Berets

posts.caption:
There are barely any snacks left on your local store rack where protein hasn’t infiltrated itself. Add to it a variety of over-the-counter supplements that people take to chase away their self-diagnosed protein deficiency, and protein consumption is at an all-time high under the guise of ‘clean’ or ‘healthy’ eating.
Swipe through for expert insights from experts on whether our collective fixation with protein could be masking a more complicated relationship with food.
Written by: Tasneem Patanwala (@tasneemxpatanwala)
#EsquireIndia

posts.caption:
“Hi Shakaila we’re xyz producers and we’d love to make a tv show with you” I’ve had a handful of those conversations over the years and they quickly go from 🤩 to 🫩
I used to feel disheartened until I looked around and saw so many examples of people not waiting for permission and instead deciding to get up and make shit happen! So that’s what I decided to do, turn this decades long dream into a reality! I pulled in every favour I had! Turned my friends into producers and show runners, begged and borrowed and you know what, it turned out pretty damn good if I do say so myself 😅
This black history month especially, I’m inspired by the talented Black British women who have shown me that it’s possible to make a mark on the small screen no matter the limitations!
I absolutely loved makeover shows growing up! But as I got older I saw how they were more brutal than beautiful!
Today, fashion psychology shows us just how deeply our clothes impact our mental health, identity, and confidence.
That’s why I’m so excited to share The Wearapy Show my new makeover series that blends fashion psychology, emotional healing and real style transformations.
We help everyday people reclaim their wardrobes after major life shifts: grief, motherhood, identity struggles, and more.
With a team of experts and no judgment, just science-backed guidance and love.
Episodes 1 and 2 launch 26th October on the Fashion is Psychology YouTube Channel. Subscribe now and follow the journey.
#bhm #wearapy #fashionpsychology #makeover
posts.alt_text:
💕💚After I went on Lorraine last year I made a silent promise to myself that I would be back at the ITV studios and I had my eyes set on @thismorning. I can’t believe just a few months later, this promise became a reality.

Of course I was low key stressing 😅😅 but there was absolutely zero reason to be nervous because the entire (and I’m not exaggerating) team were so warm and lovely that I felt like I was hanging with friends all day. 

Firstly thank you for everyone that tuned in and sent good luck vibes🍀💖! A huge shout out to @lilaflintroberts @sarah_barlow  and @amberstyledit for the opportunity to shine a light on #fashionpsychology and for allowing me to flex my styling skills. 

💃🏾🔥I couldn’t have asked for better models to showcase those amazing looks. @kate_mitchell78 @jazzifizzle53 and @misskspence you KILLT IT 

And last but certainly not least the pros @hollywilloughby and @alisonhammond55 thank you for making me feel so comfortable, it was an honour to watch you both work! 

Stay tuned for a reel on all of the BTS goss but for now I’m in Estonia about to speak on the biggest stage I have so far. Wish me luck! 🍀💖 

Dress: @fumithelabel 
Shoes: @asos 

#Tv #fashionpsychology #press #itv #thismorning

posts.caption:
Apparently wearing clothes that are more than a year old means you don’t have style.
At least that’s what someone told me after I shared my view that constantly getting bored of your clothes might not be a sign that you need more clothes, but a sign that you haven’t fully figured out your personal style yet.
Fashion psychology would suggest something very different.
One of the strongest indicators of a well-developed personal style is your ability to keep returning to the same pieces and finding new ways to enjoy them. Not because you’re forcing yourself to be sustainable. Not because you can’t afford something new.
Because those clothes genuinely feel like you.
It also suggests something else: a strong awareness of your future self.
Most people shop for the person they want to be next weekend. People with strong style identities tend to shop for the person they’ll still recognise years from now.
That’s why their wardrobes often age better.
When your clothing choices are rooted in identity rather than novelty, trends have less power over you. A garment doesn’t lose its appeal just because the algorithm has moved on.
Anyone can build a wardrobe through constant consumption.
Building a wardrobe that still excites you years later takes self-awareness.
So I’m curious…
What’s the oldest item in your wardrobe that you still wear regularly?
#FashionPsychology #PersonalStyle #overconsumption #zara
Outfit deets:
Dress: @zara
Shoes: @topshop
Flower: @londonatelierbyproduct via @wolfandbadger
Hair: @londonbraider

posts.caption:
For over six decades, Femina Miss India has been more than a pageant. It’s woven into the cultural fabric of India. It is a platform that has consistently celebrated ambition, individuality, resilience, and purpose, highlighting the inherent beauty of our women on the global stage.
This year’s winners are Femina Miss India World 2026 Sadhvi Sail, Femina Miss India 2026 – 1st Runner Up Rajnandini Pawar, and Femina Miss India 2026 – 2nd Runner Up Dr Sree Advaita, each representing modern Indian women. Together, they embody the spirit of Femina Miss India: connected by purpose, united by ambition, and ready to shape the future on their own terms.
(On the cover from L to R - Rajnandini Pawar, Sadhvi Sail, Dr Sree Advaita)

posts.caption:
#FeminaBusiness: Alia Bhatt (@aliaabhatt) extended her creative vision beyond cinema into entrepreneurship with Ed-a-Mamma, a conscious fashion brand for children and families.
Since its inception, Ed-a-Mamma has expanded from kidswear to maternity and teen collections, and in 2023, Reliance Brands acquired a majority stake, enabling wider distribution and growth. Alia remains closely involved in product ideation, brand storytelling, and strategic direction, ensuring the brand reflects her values of sustainability, inclusivity, and responsible fashion.
Ed-a-Mamma stands as a testament to how purpose-driven entrepreneurship can blend creativity, impact, and business acumen, inspiring a new generation of women-led ventures.
#Femina #AliaBhatt #EdAMamma #WomenEntrepreneurs
posts.alt_text:
For over six decades, Femina Miss India has been more than a pageant. It’s woven into the cultural fabric of India. It is a platform that has consistently celebrated ambition, individuality, resilience, and purpose, highlighting the inherent beauty of our women on the global stage. 

This year’s winners are Femina Miss India World 2026 Sadhvi Sail, Femina Miss India 2026 – 1st Runner Up Rajnandini Pawar, and Femina Miss India 2026 – 2nd Runner Up Dr Sree Advaita, each representing modern Indian women. Together, they embody the spirit of Femina Miss India: connected by purpose, united by ambition, and ready to shape the future on their own terms.

(On the cover from L to R - Rajnandini Pawar, Sadhvi Sail, Dr Sree Advaita)

posts.caption:
HUGE NEWS: Good On You has officially rated over 7,000 brands 🎉 This is a major accomplishment, as even more brands—from the small and indie through the large and luxury—are in our Directory.⁠
⁠
What goes into ratings? 🤔 A lot, actually: we have a rigorous, comprehensive system analysing up to 1,000 data points across 100 key issues to arrive at our lowest score (“We Avoid”) to our highest score (“Great”). Ratings are based solely on a brand’s transparent, public reporting that we pair with credible third-party indices, certifications, and standards.⁠
⁠
How do we select brands to rate? Well…with your help 🥹 Many of our ratings come through community requests, shared in comments and messages, which helps us understand which brands to prioritise to meet this moment. Additionally, our retail and ecommerce partners along with brands request ratings too, which is a key means to apply change to the industry from within.⁠
⁠
Another item to celebrate here? Of these rated brands, 1,000 have received our two highest scores, “Good” and “Great”. This is very exciting news, representative of how more brands are doing considering their impact on workers, the planet, and animals. (Even if that also means…6000 brands have a lot more to do to reduce their impact.)⁠
⁠
Here’s to continuing to keep people like you informed while supporting more sustainable brands that are making the difference! Cheers 🥂 Explore our 7,000 ratings at the link too ✨⁠
⁠
#SustainableFashion #FastFashion #ConsciousConsumer #EthicalConsumer #SustainableStyle #ConsciousStyle #GoodOnYou #GoodOnYouApp #EthicalFashion⁠
posts.alt_text:
Meet the top rated brands of 2025 🎊⁠
⁠
In the past year, we rated more than 1500 brands and are proud to share the ten that received our highest marks, illustrating a commitment to more sustainable practices that protect people, the planet, and animals 💚⁠
⁠
What’s a theme that unites these brands? 👀 Outside of a commitment to reducing their impact, these brands are all small and independent, a reminder of where to look in the industry for leadership and innovation. If you’re looking for a reminder to support small brands this year, this is it.⁠
⁠
Cheers to all these brands, along with all the brands that were rated “Good” and “Great” in the past year! Here’s to continuing to make a difference and for championing a better approach to what we wear. Find the full list at the link—and share which brands you’re hoping to see rated in 2026 in the comments 💬⁠
⁠
#SustainableFashion #FastFashion #SustainableBeauty #EcoBeauty #Beauty #BeautyBrand #ConsciousConsumer #EthicalConsumer #GoodOnYou #GoodOnYouApp #EthicalFashion

posts.caption:
What can be accomplished in ten years?⁠
⁠
A decade may seem short but, in reflecting on all the work we’ve done in the past decade, we’re in awe of everything that has transpired—and are grateful for the hard work of the current and former team members along with you.⁠
⁠
Whether you’ve shared our posts or have been a partner in this business, the work of creating a more sustainable future isn’t done alone but in partnership with others. These accomplishments are an example of what happens when likeminded people come together in the hopes of a better world 🌍🌎🌏⁠
⁠
Swipe to learn about ten things Good On You has accomplished in the past ten years. Share how you think the sustainable fashion and beauty industry have shifted in the past decade 💚⁠
⁠
#SustainableFashion #FastFashion #SustainableBeauty #EcoBeauty #Beauty #BeautyBrand #ConsciousConsumer #EthicalConsumer #GoodOnYou #GoodOnYouApp #EthicalFashion

posts.caption:
Since 2025, like many things, Pride hasn’t been the same as attacks on diversity, equity, and inclusion have meant less support for the LGBTQ+ community.⁠
⁠
This may seem like a siloed political situation, but know that such a problem ripples into how communities are treated—and the poor treatment of workers in supply chains. How? Diversity and sustainability intersect at a community level, resulting in dehumanizing situations like Loro Piana paying hundreds of dollars for animal fibres while greatly underpaying workers, all for sweaters that cost almost $10K USD.⁠
⁠
By disempowering vulnerable people and communities, economic power is removed. Your clothes are a representation of this, which is why it’s integral to support brands that not only support communities like the queer community but who are doing right by workers. These actions go hand-in-hand.⁠
⁠
Companies and leadership quitting Pride is worrisome as it relates to freedoms but also that it ripples into businesses rolling back progress, creating covers for waste, economic inequality, gender and sexual disparities, and other harmful business practices. Remember this, when the need arises to make a purchase 👀⁠
⁠
Here’s to making change that benefits all communities, and to a happy Pride 🏳️‍🌈 Learn more about these issues and how problems like pinkhushing turn back the clock of progress⁠ ↑⁠
⁠
#Pride #LGBTQ #Pride2026 #Sustainability #SustainableFashion

posts.caption:
Presenting one of our three Summer issue covers with the leading men of @theodysseymovie: Matt Damon as Odysseus.
A Hollywood veteran of nearly five decades now, Damon has worked on approximately 80 film sets. And yet, as he told GQ’s @zachbaron, “Every single location on this movie would’ve been the hardest location on any other movie I’ve ever done… Every time we’d go somewhere, we’d be like, ‘Well, Iceland will be easier.’ And then it’s raining sideways and it’s fucking freezing. Iceland was like, ‘Yeah, easy? Hey, hold my beer.’”
For Damon, though, the challenge was part of what made the project so special: “All of the things that might have been difficult for me at an earlier point in my life weren’t difficult. They became fun. Like being wet and cold was not a hardship. It was actually something I felt really lucky to be experiencing. It felt temporary and like a gift.”
Damon unwraps the ‘gift’ that was making The Odyssey, alongside his co-stars Robert Pattinson and Tom Holland, and director Christopher Nolan, for GQ’s summer issue cover story at the 🔗 in bio.
Story by @zachbaron
Photographs by @alexprager
Styled by @georgecortina
Hair by @teddycharleshair at Nevermind
Skin by @jostrettell at Walter Schupfer Management
Set design by @joneslikeindiana
Tailoring by Yelena Travkina and Susanna Badalyan
Key Gaffer by @mick411
Key Grip by @clayton_n_iii
Retouching by @bermudezstudio
Modeling by Kara Smatt
Produced by @campproductions
posts.alt_text:
Presenting one of our three Summer issue covers with the leading men of @theodysseymovie: Robert Pattinson as Antinous. 

Pattinson’s part in The Odyssey is not big—he plays the cockiest of the many suitors who have installed themselves at Ithaca, seeking the hand of Penelope (played by Anne Hathaway) while Matt Damon’s Odysseus is away. “My character’s a little like James Woods in Casino. That was my inspiration for it,” Pattinson said. “He’s like a little sleazy. I kept saying at my costume fitting, ‘I really want to have leopard underpants.’”

Pattinson talks to GQ’s @zachbaron about teaming up with Nolan again for The Odyssey, alongside co-stars Matt Damon and Tom Holland, at the 🔗 in bio.

Story by @zachbaron
Photographs by @alexprager
Styled by @georgecortina
Hair by @teddycharleshair at Nevermind

posts.caption:
Presenting one of our three Summer issue covers with the leading men of @theodysseymovie: Tom Holland as Telemachus.
When Christopher Nolan first offered Holland a part in The Odyssey, the production dates were identical to those of Spider-Man: Brand New Day (and Holland’s commitments to the Spider-Man franchise are ironclad). “I said to Chris, like, ‘Look, I want to do this movie, but if I’m going to do it, I’m going to have to call Sony and have a very uncomfortable conversation,’ ” Holland said. Then he immediately had a very uncomfortable conversation.
“Chris has that reputation of ‘This movie isn’t going to go five months over, and we aren’t actually going to lose Tom for two years,’ ” Holland said. “Any other director, it might have been a slightly different conversation.” The Odyssey started on the day Nolan said it would and finished nine days early.
For our summer cover story, Holland talks about coming of age on set, getting to star alongside his partner on another project, and how he’s approaching the future of his career at the 🔗 in bio.
Story by @zachbaron
Photographs by @alexprager
Styled by @georgecortina
Hair by @teddycharleshair at Nevermind
